title: Frequent colds and immunity
excerpt: If you seem to catch every cold going around, here is how Ayurveda understands weak resistance and the simple daily habits the tradition suggests to support the body's resilience and vitality.
body_html:
why you keep catching it

You seem to catch every cold that passes through the house. A colleague sniffles on Monday and by Wednesday it is your turn. The runny nose, the heavy head, the tiredness that lingers a few days too long. If this is you, you are not imagining it, and you are not alone. Some people simply seem to fall ill more easily than others, and it can be quietly demoralising.

Ayurveda has a calm, practical way of looking at this. It speaks of vyadhikshamatva, the body's own capacity to withstand illness. The Caraka Samhita puts it plainly: people who are weak, who eat poorly, and who are mentally drained cope poorly when illness comes, while those with the opposite qualities hold up more easily (Caraka Samhita, Sutrasthana 28.7). In other words, what matters here is not a single thing you buy or swallow. It is your overall inner strength, or bala.

what matters here is not a single thing you buy or swallow

what really runs you down

The tradition adds a useful detail. Strength is described as being of three kinds: the strength you are simply born with, the strength that rises and falls with the seasons and your age, and the strength you build through your own diet and daily habits (Caraka Samhita, Sutrasthana 11.36). The first two you cannot change much. The third one is entirely in your hands, and that is where everyday effort goes.

Behind a run-down feeling, classical texts point to something called ojas, described as the very first essence formed in the body, the quiet substrate of vitality (Caraka Samhita, Sutrasthana 17.75). When this gets depleted, the texts describe a familiar picture: tiredness, low spirits, constant worry, dryness, and a body that feels easily run down (Caraka Samhita, Sutrasthana 17.73). Many people who keep falling ill will recognise that state. The supportive approach, then, is less about chasing any one ailment and more about supporting the body's own resilience and vitality, ojas and bala, so the body can do its own work.

the habits that help

Here are a few gentle, everyday habits the tradition leans on. Treat them as supportive routines for everyday wellbeing, not cures or a shield against illness.

Eat warm, freshly cooked, easy-to-digest food at regular times, and eat enough. The Caraka Samhita specifically links inadequate eating to low strength (Sutrasthana 28.7), so skipping meals or living on cold leftovers works against you. A simple bowl of warm dal and rice, or soft cooked vegetables with a little ghee, is the kind of nourishing food the tradition favours.

Protect your sleep and your routine. The texts treat strength as something built steadily through regimen, not in one heroic burst (Caraka Samhita, Sutrasthana 11.36). Going to bed at a consistent hour, getting morning light, and not pushing yourself to exhaustion all help your body rebuild its reserves.

Make room for calm. Ayurveda places ojas in the region of the heart, the seat of steadiness and vitality (Caraka Samhita, Sutrasthana 30.7-8), and it counts constant worry among the signs of depleted strength. A few quiet minutes of slow breathing, a short walk, or simply sitting without your phone are small daily practices that support this. The Ashtanga Hridaya describes ojas in similar terms, as the essence that sustains the whole body (Sutrasthana 11.37-38).

The tradition also speaks of rasayana, the idea of nourishing and renewing your vitality over time so the healthy stay strong, not only tending to those already unwell (Caraka Samhita, Chikitsasthana 1.1.4). For most people this is best understood not as a single tonic but as the slow, cumulative effect of eating well, resting well, and staying calm.

when to see a doctor

One honest note. Frequent infections can sometimes point to something a routine cannot address. If your colds are severe, keep returning, drag on for weeks, or come with fever, breathlessness, weight loss, or deep fatigue, please see a doctor or a qualified vaidya. This article is general information drawn from classical texts, not a diagnosis or a substitute for medical care.

The reassuring part is how ordinary these habits are. Warm food, steady sleep, a quieter mind. None of it is dramatic, and that is rather the point. You support the body's resilience and vitality the way the texts describe building strength, a little each day, until the body simply has more to draw on.

Try This Today

  • Eat warm and regular: Eat warm, freshly cooked, easy-to-digest food at regular times, and eat enough; a bowl of warm dal and rice or soft vegetables with a little ghee.
  • Protect your sleep and routine: Go to bed at a consistent hour, get morning light, and don't push yourself to exhaustion, so the body rebuilds its reserves.
  • Make room for calm: A few quiet minutes of slow breathing, a short walk, or sitting without your phone to ease the constant worry that signals depleted strength.
author: Kerala Ayurveda Editorial Team

metafields.dek: If you seem to catch every cold going around, here is how Ayurveda understands weak resistance and the simple daily habits the tradition suggests to support the body's resilience and vitality.
metafields.disclaimer: Ayurveda guidance on AyurHQ is supportive care, not emergency or diagnostic medical treatment.
at_a_glance: Resistance to illness, vyadhikshamatva, rests on your overall inner strength (bala), not any single remedy.
Of the three kinds of strength, the one built through diet and daily habit is entirely in your hands.
Warm food, steady sleep, and a calmer mind support ojas and bala a little each day.

glossary_terms.definition: the body's own capacity to withstand illness
overall inner strength; the foundation of resistance to disease
the first essence formed in the body, the quiet substrate of vitality
nourishing and renewing vitality over time so the healthy stay strong

faqs.question: What is the meaning of "vyadhikshamatva" in Ayurveda?
faqs.answer: In the Ayurvedic tradition, vyadhikshamatva is defined as: the body's own capacity to withstand illness

faqs.question: How can I start addressing this issue today?
faqs.answer: One of the most recommended practices is to eat warm and regular. Eat warm, freshly cooked, easy-to-digest food at regular times, and eat enough; a bowl of warm dal and rice or soft vegetables with a little ghee.

faqs.question: Are there any specific lifestyle habits I should adopt?
faqs.answer: A key suggestion is to protect your sleep and routine. Go to bed at a consistent hour, get morning light, and don't push yourself to exhaustion, so the body rebuilds its reserves.
